fr:mainmatter.fr:p: When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.

Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{B} and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{A}, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon , we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.

An inverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f\circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with an inverse as an invertible function.
A function f\colon A\to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x=y\to fx=fy is invertible.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.
For any function f\colon A\to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g =1_{B}\right \rparen \mathclose {} be a section of a function f\colon A\to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ P &\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.

fr:title.text: N-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by and respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by ) a retract of su=_Asv.

Let A be n-truncated and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and . In the inductive step, we need to show that for all u,v\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type u=v is n+1-truncated assuming the same of all the identification types of A and Bx for x:A. By , u=v is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} p_*u.2=v.2 and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis; thus the result follows from .

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .
Let s\colon A\to B exhibit A as a retract of B; then \mathsf {act}_{s}\colon x = y\to sx=sy exhibits x=y as a retract of sx=sy for each x,y:A.

fr:title.text: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any right-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

for x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} , the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{x}{b}\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}b is given by the component \Phi _{\mathcal {B}}xb\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B} x} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}^*b of the unit law of the given cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of left cleavings (). Fixing a: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} , we must show that the reflexive graph structure on \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} with edges in b \approx _{\mathcal {B} a} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b' and reflexivity data for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b' is a proposition. As each \mathcal {B} a is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of \mathcal {B} a. Therefore, we conclude via .
Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via and .
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

fr:title.text: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use and .

fr:title.text: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let B be a type. Given a quasi-inverse function g\colon B\to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} , we can restrict the path object \mathcal {A} to a path object structure g^*\mathcal {A} on B as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {}\left \lvert g^*\mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} &:\equiv B\\ x \approx _{g^*\mathcal {A}} y &:\equiv gx \approx _{\mathcal {A}} gy\\ \mathsf {rx}_{g^*\mathcal {A}}x &:\equiv \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}} \mathopen {}\left \lparen gx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Fixing x:B, we must show that \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}gx\approx _{\mathcal {A}}gy is a proposition. But this is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}gx\approx _{\mathcal {A}}y, which is a proposition by the univalence of \mathcal {A}; thus the result follows from the closure of propositions under retracts ().
